dateStatements.notes: The date applies to current catalog items 2–4 and Scribe A, not uniformly to all later contents or the early-sixteenth-century composite binding.

entities.description: Composite paper codex of ten or eleven fascicles, bound circa 1504–1512 for T. Mylle and later owned and annotated by John Stow. In booklet 1, one anonymous Secretary hand copies the three CME00112 components circa 1465 on ff. 1r–46r: the abridged Brut at ff. 1r–17v, Lydgate at ff. 17v–18v, and the unique London Chronicle version at ff. 18v–46r.
entities.label: London, Lambeth Palace Library, MS 306

notes: Blind-stamped leather over wooden boards, rebacked; clasps missing.
Booklet 1 is ff. 1–48; items 2–4 are all assigned to Scribe A.

rights.basis: The textual content was copied circa 1465 and predates modern copyright terms by centuries; this does not determine rights in modern representations.
rights.component: Medieval Middle English textual content on ff. 1r–46r, excluding modern editions, encodings, catalog data, and photographs

reviewNotes: Gairdner's 1880 edition is the immediate source for U-M CME00112; Lambeth MS 306 is the edition's direct manuscript source. The relation is supported at both printed-page and folio levels.
The complete Lambeth facsimile and complete Gairdner volume are preserved as full objects. Narrow work associations are carried by work_portion locators rather than by cropping the cached sources.
The three medieval components remain distinct even though Gairdner supplied one collective section heading and U-M used only two DIV2 elements.
Public-domain medieval text, public-domain 1880 edition, U-M's NoC-US assertion, Internet Archive file metadata, and Lambeth photograph permissions are separately scoped rights statements.
No access barrier was bypassed. Direct public IIIF resources were used; the viewer's verification interstitial is recorded rather than circumvented.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of U-M CME00112, which encodes James Gairdner's pp. 1–80 transcription in the 1880 Camden Society volume Three Fifteenth-Century Chronicles. Gairdner transcribed Lambeth Palace Library MS 306, ff. 1r–46r: an abridged Brut, John Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England copied as prose, and a London civic chronicle through 1465. The complete 1880 source volume is publicly downloadable and cached. Lambeth's complete 415-canvas manuscript facsimile is also publicly accessible and cached as a whole-object research bundle; exact work coverage is confirmed at canvases 5–95. Public access to Lambeth's photographs does not supply an open reuse license, and the provider's current forms require separate permission for publication or redistribution.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous abridger and London chronicler; John Lydgate for the Verses on the Kings of England
catalogSummary.date: The three manuscript components were copied circa 1465; the edited sequence was first published in 1880
catalogSummary.region: England, with London specifically associated with the civic chronicle; exact place of compilation unknown
catalogSummary.title: A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306

catalogingSubject.label: A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the manuscript-specific sequence copied by one fifteenth-century hand at Lambeth Palace Library MS 306, ff. 1r–46r, printed by James Gairdner on pp. 1–80 of Three Fifteenth-Century Chronicles and encoded as CME00112. Despite Gairdner's collective editorial title and the XML's two DIV2 elements, the sequence contains three intellectual works: an abridged prose Brut, John Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England copied as prose, and a London civic chronicle for 1189–1465. The record does not flatten those works into one authorial chronicle and keeps Gairdner's notes, the 1880 edition, the U-M encoding, the repository XML, the physical manuscript, and its digital photographs at their proper layers.

contentParts.label: Cronycullys of Englonde: abridged Brut
notes: Folio 17v and IIIF canvas 38 are shared with the following Lydgate component.
contentParts.summary: A unique abbreviated form of the Prose Brut's Peculiar Version, from legendary British origins through the first year of Henry IV.

contentParts.label: John Lydgate, Verses on the Kings of England (to Henry VI)
notes: The witness form and XML element choice are prose, but the intellectual work remains verse.
Folio 18v and IIIF canvas 40 are shared with the London chronicle transition.
contentParts.summary: Fifteen reign stanzas from William I through Henry VI, copied and encoded as prose; DIMEV record 5731 / IMEV-NIMEV 3632.

contentParts.label: London Chronicle, 1189–1465

locators.value: /ETS/EEBO/TEXT/BODY/DIV1/DIV2[2]/P[16] through the end (295 P, 134 TABLE, 7 LG, 19 L)

notes: The LG and L nodes format civic officer lists and do not change the part's prose form.
Canvas 95 / f. 46r is the confirmed endpoint; canvas 96 / f. 46v begins later John Stow material outside CME00112.
contentParts.summary: A unique civic chronicle version arranged by regnal and mayoral years, beginning with keepers and bailiffs under Richard I and ending in Edward IV's fourth year.

contentParts.label: Gairdner's editorial and manuscript-note apparatus
notes: Some NOTE content reports manuscript marginalia or later corrector interventions, but the encoded NOTE representation belongs to the edition/encoding layer rather than increasing the number of medieval works.
contentParts.summary: Interleaved footnotes and marginal-note transcriptions identifying readings, corrections, later hands, dates, people, and places.

assertion.notes: The current catalog dates items 2–4 and their common scribal hand circa 1465. This is surviving witness production, not proof that the three intellectual works were first composed or compiled together then. No artificial exact normalized year is assigned to the circa statement.

dateStatements.display: Surviving Lambeth MS 306 copies of all three consecutive components: circa 1465
Legendary British origins through 1465; the civic chronicle runs from Richard I's reign to Edward IV's fourth regnal year

assertion.notes: The directly inspected title page and Roman date M.DCCC.LXXX govern. U-M's 1928 statement and Cambridge Core's 1881 volume date are retained as source discrepancies rather than substituted.

dateStatements.display: First published by the Camden Society in 1880

extent.description: Four cataloging parts are modeled: three medieval works and Gairdner's interleaved prose apparatus. The medieval sequence consists of two prose chronicles and one fifteen-stanza verse chronicle. Exact verse-line extent is left null because this Lambeth copy rewrites or runs the verse as prose and the XML supplies no recoverable lineation. Raw XML counts are 406 P, 273 NOTE, 134 TABLE, 80 PB, 7 LG, and 19 L; the seven LG and nineteen L elements format civic lists inside the prose London chronicle.

formStatements.description: Two prose chronicles surround a verse chronicle that the Lambeth witness and XML render as fifteen prose paragraphs.
A generally fifteen-stanza rhyme-royal poem, copied continuously as prose in this witness and encoded as fifteen P elements.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The XML declares enm. Gairdner's modern editorial prose and Latin marginal annotations are representation-layer matter and do not become additional work languages.
The three medieval works are cataloged separately inside one manuscript-specific composite because both Gairdner and the current Lambeth catalog independently support that boundary.
Shared folios and canvases at ff. 17v and 18v are intentional boundary surfaces, not duplicate or uncertain coverage.

The complete Lambeth facsimile is public to view and cached for private research, but its photographs have no verified open license; this metadata record does not imply permission to redistribute them.
No author, editor, publication date, physical witness, digital provider, or photographic-rights statement is allowed to migrate across layers without explicit evidence.

next_steps: Consult current codicological and palaeographical scholarship on Lambeth MS 306 booklet 1 and its paper stocks.
Separate evidence for scribal employment, exemplar provenance, compilation place, and authorial activity rather than deriving one from another.
Retain England as broad cultural origin and London as a subject association until direct production evidence supports a narrower place.

openQuestions.question: How precisely does the Lambeth prose-rendered and partly rewritten copy relate to the fifteen-stanza textual tradition of Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England?

notes: Middle English language and English historical-cultural content support this broad association, but the current catalog does not firmly localize production. This is not an exact place where the composite or its three components were compiled.
The civic chronicle is organized by London keepers, bailiffs, mayors, and sheriffs. The association is not converted into an unsupported place-of-compilation claim.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous abridger of the Lambeth MS 306 Brut

assertion.notes: The catalog identifies the component as an abbreviated Peculiar Version of the Brut and says scribal errors show that the surviving copy is not an authorial original.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous compiler and continuator of the Lambeth MS 306 London chronicle

assertion.notes: The witness preserves a unique London Chronicle version through 1465; no named author or compiler is supplied.
One hand copies the three components consecutively in the first booklet; this does not prove that the scribe composed or intellectually compiled all three.

summaries.value: A manuscript-specific historical sequence surviving in copies made circa 1465. It opens with an abbreviated prose Brut running from the legendary daughters of Diocletian and Brutus through the first year of Henry IV. It then copies John Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England through Henry VI as prose, followed without a new XML division by a London civic chronicle organized under keepers, bailiffs, mayors, and sheriffs from Richard I through Edward IV's fourth year. The final entries report the capture of Henry VI and Edward IV's new coinage. James Gairdner first printed the sequence in 1880.

Gairdner says the sequence looks like one chronicle but is really three short chronicles written or transcribed consecutively by the same pen. The current Lambeth catalog independently describes them as items 2–4: the abbreviated Brut on ff. 1r–17v, Lydgate's Verses on ff. 17v–18v, and the unique London Chronicle version on ff. 18v–46r. Shared folios at both transitions are retained rather than forcing false clean leaf boundaries.

CME00112 contains one DIV1 and only two DIV2 elements. DIV2[1] is the Brut. DIV2[2] joins the fifteen prose-encoded Lydgate paragraphs to the London chronicle beginning at P[16]. Across the XML there are 406 P, 273 NOTE, 134 TABLE, 80 PB, 7 LG, and 19 L elements. The LG and L nodes occur in civic officer-list formatting inside the London chronicle; they are not evidence that the civic chronicle is verse. Gairdner's interleaved notes are modeled as editorial apparatus rather than medieval work content.

The complete source scan's title page dates the Camden Society volume M.DCCC.LXXX (1880), and that date governs this record. The current U-M item says 1928, while the current Cambridge Core section page associates the section with a September 1881 volume date. Those later catalog and platform assertions are retained as unresolved descriptive discrepancies, not silently merged with the inspected publication statement.

assertion.notes: Gairdner's established editorial heading names the complete printed and encoded sequence, not one medieval witness rubric.

titles.value: A Short English Chronicle, from Lambeth MS. 306

notes: The rubric belongs only to the abridged Brut on ff. 1r–17v, not to the Lydgate verses or London chronicle that follow.

titles.value: In the noble londe of Surrye was some tyme a greate kynge and a myghty that was named Dioclesyan

notes: This is the witness heading above the Lydgate component, not a title for the complete composite.

titles.value: Verses on the Kings of England (to Henry VI)
This myghti William, Duke of Normandie, as bokes olde maketh mencion
The names of the Kepers and Baylyffes of the Cite of London in the tyme of Kynge Richarde the fyrst

work.summary: A manuscript-specific historical sequence surviving in copies made circa 1465. It opens with an abbreviated prose Brut running from the legendary daughters of Diocletian and Brutus through the first year of Henry IV. It then copies John Lydgate's Verses on the Kings of England through Henry VI as prose, followed without a new XML division by a London civic chronicle organized under keepers, bailiffs, mayors, and sheriffs from Richard I through Edward IV's fourth year. The final entries report the capture of Henry VI and Edward IV's new coinage. James Gairdner first printed the sequence in 1880.
